messages.content:
### Lesson Summary: Quality Management and Accounting in Service Industries

This study focuses on the intersection of quality management and accounting, specifically addressing the research gap regarding **quality cost calculation** within service enterprises. 

#### Key Points:

*   **Rationale for Quality Costing:** In a globalized market, enterprises must optimize management efficiency to remain competitive. Quality cost calculation serves as a vital diagnostic tool to assess, control, and improve these management systems (p. S.2).
*   **The Service Sector Challenge:** Most existing quality cost models were developed for manufacturing. Service industries, which account for a significant portion of modern global economies, require specialized models due to the unique nature of their processes (pp. S.15, S.16).
*   **Defining Quality Costs:** There is no single, universal definition; however, across literature, quality costs are generally viewed as expenditures incurred to prevent, evaluate, and address defects, as well as the losses stemming from internal and external deficiencies (pp. S.17–S.19).
*   **The Proposed Model:** The authors introduce an original, interdisciplinary model tailored for service firms. This model moves beyond traditional structures by utilizing:
    *   A **process-based approach** (mapping service delivery phases from planning to sales) (pp. S.12, S.16).
    *   **Process matrices and budgets** to identify specific cost drivers (p. S.16).
*   **Empirical Validation:** The book provides a practical verification of this model through research in selected service enterprises, demonstrating how quality costing can be used to identify organizational weaknesses and optimize economic and non-economic efficiency (pp. S.13, S.16).
*   **Target Audience:** This work serves both academics and practitioners, offering a methodological bridge between quality management and managerial accounting (pp. S.2, S.13).

Lesson 2: Quality Management and Accounting in Service Industries - Presentation7 slides
Quality Management and Accounting in Service Industries
  • Global competition requires improved management system efficiency.
  • Quality cost calculation helps assess and maintain competitiveness.
  • The book addresses the research gap for service companies.

This book, *Quality Management and Accounting in Service Industries: A New Model of Quality Cost Calculation* by Wojciech Sadkowski and Piotr Jedynak, explores the integration of quality management and accounting specifically tailored to the service sector.

Key points of the text include:

Purpose and Motivation
The authors address a research gap by developing a framework to assess the efficiency of management systems in service-oriented enterprises. As global competition intensifies, service companies require precise tools to maintain competitiveness, which the authors argue is achieved through effective quality cost calculation.

Book Structure
The work is divided into a theoretical section and a practical section:
The theoretical portion reviews the history of quality cost accounting, current models, and the unique operational requirements of service firms.
The practical portion introduces the authors' original model for quality cost calculation, detailing the assumptions, cost structures, and methodology validated through empirical research in a selected service company.

The Authors' Model
A central contribution of the book is the creation of a new model specifically designed for service industries. The model incorporates process-based approaches and specific cost structures (such as planning, procurement, delivery, and sales phases) to help managers quantify and analyze quality-related expenditures.

Target Audience
The text serves as a professional resource for practitioners in quality management and accounting, consultants, and academics, including master's and doctoral students (p. S.2, S.13).

Resources and Methodology
The book includes comprehensive support materials, such as a list of figures and tables, which detail the mathematical and logical mapping of quality costs ($Figure 6.1$, $Table 4.8$). The methodology utilized to verify the model is documented through research procedures and empirical case studies (p. S.8, S.12).

Funding
The project was supported by the Priority Research Area Society of the Future, under the "Excellence Initiative – Research University" program at Jagiellonian University (p. S.13).

This text introduces a book focused on applying quality cost accounting to service enterprises to improve management system efficiency. The key points are summarized below:

**Context and Motivation**
Global market competition necessitates more efficient management systems for business entities to remain competitive. While quality cost accounting is established in the production sector, it is historically underutilized in service enterprises despite the growing economic importance of the service sector (p. 15).

**Theoretical Framework**
The first three chapters provide a foundation for this approach:
- Chapter 1 reviews definitions, classifications, and the evolution of quality costs within management sciences.
- Chapter 2 evaluates existing structural and activity-based models of quality cost accounting.
- Chapter 3 analyzes the specific nature of service enterprises, including service quality management, measurement methods, and the identification of quality cost determinants (p. 15-16).

**Empirical Application**
The latter three chapters detail the practical implementation of the authors' original model:
- Chapter 4 outlines the construction of their model, introducing specific tools such as a process matrix and a process budget of quality costs, as well as a procedure based on a modified Bernatene-Grün diagram.
- Chapter 5 details the empirical research methodology.
- Chapter 6 presents the verification of the model through case studies in service companies, evaluating its results, potential, and limitations in improving management system efficiency (p. 16).

This section explores the evolution and definition of "quality costs" within management sciences. The key points are summarized below:

**The Nature of Quality**
Quality is an interdisciplinary concept lacking a single, unambiguous definition. Historically rooted in philosophical inquiry—ranging from Plato’s "degree of perfection" to Aristotle’s "inherent categories"—it is now viewed in modern business as the degree to which inherent characteristics meet customer requirements. Quality is recognized as a fundamental driver of enterprise success in competitive global markets.

**Historical Context and Development**
The focus on quality costs emerged in the 1940s alongside the surge in military production demand, where high defect rates highlighted the need for measurement. 
* 1940s: Formulation of internal costs (defects detected during production) and external costs (defects detected by users).
* 1950s: J.M. Juran and A.V. Feigenbaum introduced quality costs into scientific literature, establishing them as a vital tool for quality control.
* 1967: The American Quality Control Association formalized cost classifications (preventive measures, assessment/appraisal, internal losses, and external losses).
* 1987: The ISO 9000 series significantly increased the importance of quality cost management globally (p. 18, 19).

**Defining Quality Costs**
While definitions vary by author and decade, they generally converge on three themes:
1. Costs of achieving a specific quality level (prevention and appraisal).
2. Costs incurred due to failing to achieve that level (internal and external failures).
3. The difference between actual production costs and the costs of an ideal, error-free process ($Cost_{actual} - Cost_{ideal} = Quality Cost$) (p. 20-22).

**Strategic Importance**
In management, quality costs function as:
* A diagnostic indicator to identify process weaknesses (p. 21, 23).
* A financial instrument to bridge the gap between technical operations and executive management language (p. 22, 23).
* A strategic resource for reducing total production costs by highlighting areas for systemic improvement (p. 23). 

Ultimately, practitioners define these costs as a measurement of the efficiency of an organization’s quality management system, where the goal is to reach a state of error-free operation (p. 21, 23).

This section provides a comprehensive review of existing quality cost accounting models and their theoretical frameworks, specifically within the context of quality management science.

Key Points:

Evolution of Quality Cost Models: The text documents the progression of quality cost accounting through various frameworks, noting both early structural models (such as the PAF model, Feigenbaum’s model, and the ASQC model) and later iterations, including those developed by John Bank, E. Kindlarski, M. Czajkowski, and the process model of quality costs.

Comparative Analysis: The authors present a detailed review of diverse models, systematically categorized by their cost structures and applications. This includes a clear distinction between cost models that emphasize traditional structural approaches (such as the BS 6143 standard) and those that incorporate more contemporary, process-oriented views of service provision.

Role of Z. Zymonik’s Framework: A significant portion of the analysis centers on the specific contributions of Z. Zymonik’s model, which serves as a notable reference point for understanding how management systems integrate quality costs to assess efficiency.

Integration of Standards and Tools: The review highlights the shift toward using quality cost accounting not merely as a ledger of expenses, but as a management tool. Various models attempt to quantify the relationship between quality management expenditures and overall enterprise efficiency, often utilizing metrics to evaluate organizational performance.

Methodological Variety: The researchers emphasize that there is no singular universal model, but rather a spectrum of approaches tailored to different organizational needs. These models range from simple cost-categorization schemes to complex matrices that map quality costs directly onto specific business processes.

In summary, these chapters establish the theoretical foundation needed to justify the authors’ own proposed model, highlighting the necessity of shifting toward models that better capture the unique operational nuances of service-based businesses.

This section of the book (pp. 94–134) focuses on the specific operational characteristics of service enterprises and how these factors influence the management of quality costs. The key findings and points are structured as follows:

**1. The Nature of Services**
The authors define services as distinct from physical products, emphasizing their intangibility, inseparability, perishability, and variability. These unique attributes necessitate specific approaches to quality management that deviate from traditional manufacturing-based quality control (p. 94–95).

**2. Service Process Mapping**
A critical component of this chapter is the distinction between service processes. The authors provide a map of service provision (p. 103) and categorize these processes using standard frameworks like the Process Classification Framework (PCF) to create a hierarchical structure (p. 106). This allows for a granular analysis of where quality costs originate within a service organization.

**3. Quality Management in Services**
The text explores specialized frameworks for managing quality in service environments, such as Ch.-Ch. Yang’s quality management system (p. 113). Various measurement methods are discussed to address the challenge of quantifying the quality of non-tangible outputs, alongside essential tools for analyzing quality management (p. 115–118).

**4. Quality Cost Occurrence**
The authors examine the determinants of quality cost structures in service sectors (p. 121), highlighting how costs are tied to specific service delivery phases. A core contribution is the conceptual scheme of how quality costs consistently occur throughout service lifecycles (p. 123). By identifying the specific processes that drive these costs (p. 125), the authors argue that enterprises can better isolate, measure, and minimize inefficiencies.

**5. Theoretical Implications**
The section synthesizes academic definitions of "service" and creates a foundation for building a cost-accounting model. The primary argument is that because service quality is tied to the interaction between the service provider and the customer, the cost-accounting system must reflect the fluidity of these operations rather than relying on static, industrial-era cost models.

This section (pp. 135-156) introduces an original quality cost accounting model specifically tailored for service enterprises, addressing the research gap in applying these methodologies to non-manufacturing industries.

Key components of the model include:

Definition and Assumptions
The authors propose a specialized framework to quantify and manage quality-related expenses. The model is built upon established theoretical foundations, incorporating specific service industry characteristics such as intangible output and customer-process interaction. The classification of costs is distributed across business phases: planning, procurement, delivery, and sales.

Structural Framework
The quality costs within service organizations are organized by both process phase and functional activity. The authors provide a matrix-based approach to cost allocation:
1. Planning Phase: Focused on upstream design costs to prevent errors.
2. Procurement Phase: Monitoring the quality of external inputs.
3. Service Delivery Phase: Capturing real-time costs during the interaction between the service provider and the client.
4. Sales Phase: Addressing post-delivery quality concerns.

Implementation Methodology
The framework utilizes a process-oriented budget structure. To integrate this into management systems, the authors employ a modified version of the Bernatene-Grün diagram. This visual and analytical tool aids in the procedural mapping of costs, which is summarized by the relationship between quality-related activities ($Q_{a}$) and total process efficiency ($E_{p}$). The utility of the model is centered on the following logical flow:
- Identifying specific service processes.
- Assigning costs to those processes.
- Evaluating the impact on organizational efficiency.

The model serves as a practical tool for managers to distinguish between the costs of prevention, appraisal, and failure within a service context, ultimately allowing for a better assessment of the efficiency of internal management systems.

This section details the empirical research methodology employed to verify the proposed quality cost accounting model within a service enterprise.

Research Framework and Methodology
The research follows a structured procedure (p. 157) designed to test the authors' cost accounting concepts in a practical setting. The methodology centers on an organizational case study (p. 161), utilizing the internal processes of a specific service firm as the empirical basis.

Organizational and Process Analysis
The study begins by analyzing the organizational structure of the participant enterprise (p. 161). Researchers examined the firm's specific functional hierarchy and mapped its operational processes (p. 163). This mapping is critical for identifying exactly where quality costs are generated within the service delivery cycle. According to the research, the identified processes are categorized to allow for a precise calculation of cost centers, ensuring that the model accounts for the unique characteristics of service-based operations (p. 165).

Objectives of the Empirical Phase
The key focus of this section is to transform theoretical quality cost frameworks into actionable data. By defining the operational map, the researchers established a baseline for measuring the efficiency of management systems. This process-oriented approach (p. 164) serves as the foundation for the subsequent verification phase, where the model is validated against real-world economic data.

This section (pp. 169–198) provides the empirical verification of the proposed quality cost accounting model within a selected service enterprise, bridging the gap between theoretical frameworks and economic practice.

### Key Objectives and Methodology
The authors apply their developed model to a specific service company to evaluate the efficiency of its management systems. The research methodology involves:
* Establishing a baseline of quality cost estimation for the year 2019.
* Categorizing costs across four distinct phases: planning, procurement, service delivery, and sales.
* Developing a process matrix to map how quality costs are incurred across organizational operations.

### Analysis and Tools
The study utilizes several quantitative tools to analyze the gathered data, focusing on:
* **Cost Structure Mapping:** Identifying the financial impact of quality-related activities within specific service phases.
* **Pareto-Lorenz Diagrams:** Used to visualize and prioritize prevention costs and irregularities, highlighting the areas where management can most effectively gain efficiencies.
* **Ratio Analysis:** Employed to interpret the magnitude and significance of quality costs relative to the company's overall operational performance.
* **Process Budgeting:** Constructing a 2020 quality cost budget based on the findings from the previous year to demonstrate the predictive and corrective utility of the model.

### Findings and Conclusions
The research confirms that systematic quality cost calculation serves as a vital tool for economic efficiency. By monitoring these costs through a process-oriented approach, the enterprise can:
* Identify specific "iceberg" costs that are often hidden in service operations.
* Transition from reactive error correction to a proactive management stance.
* Quantify the efficiency of their management systems, as shown by the comparative results between the 2019 estimation and the 2020 budget implementation.

Ultimately, this chapter serves as evidence that the authors’ model is capable of being integrated into actual business environments, providing practitioners with a structured method to monitor, control, and reduce quality-related financial losses.

The document concludes (p. 199–204) by summarizing the research and practical findings regarding quality cost calculation in service industries. The core points are as follows:

The book addresses a significant research gap by providing a tailored methodology for assessing management efficiency in service enterprises. Because services differ inherently from manufacturing, standard quality cost models are often inadequate. The authors propose a specific model based on a process-oriented approach, which allows companies to identify, measure, and analyze costs associated with quality activities.

The empirical verification of this model in a selected service entity demonstrates that quality cost calculation serves as a vital tool for economic practice. By applying this methodology, organizations can identify cost-intensive areas, optimize internal processes, and ultimately improve the efficiency of their management systems. 

Key takeaways include:

1. Theoretical Foundation: The research establishes that quality cost management, when properly integrated into accounting practices, directly improves the competitive position and operational efficiency of service enterprises.
2. Practical Application: Through the application of the process matrix and quality cost budgeting, firms can gain transparency regarding where quality costs originate (e.g., procurement, service delivery, or sales phases).
3. Strategic Utility: The model provides decision-makers with quantitative data necessary for assessing the performance of management systems, moving beyond qualitative assessments toward evidence-based management.
4. Future Implications: This work serves as a foundational resource for practitioners and academics seeking to implement rigorous cost-accounting systems in the service sector, emphasizing the necessity of adapting traditional accounting frameworks to the specific operational characteristics of service provision.

The findings highlight that the systematic monitoring of quality costs is not merely an accounting exercise but a strategic requirement for organizations striving to maintain competitiveness in global markets.

Based on the provided document, the area covering pages 205–240 (which corresponds to the Appendix and Index) focuses on the supplemental data and organizational reference material for the research presented in the book.

Specifically, the concluding sections contain the following:

Appendix 1. Annex (p. 205)
The Annex serves as a repository for the detailed supporting data utilized in the authors' empirical study. While the specific data tables are not explicitly detailed in the text provided, this section is intended to provide transparency and verification for the quality cost calculations applied to the service enterprise discussed in previous chapters. It houses the primary datasets that allowed the authors to perform the process-based quality cost analysis and the subsequent ratio analysis.

Index (p. 225)
The Index provides a comprehensive alphabetical guide to key terms, concepts, and methodologies discussed throughout the book. It serves as a tool for readers to navigate topics such as:
1. Quality cost accounting models (including PAF, Zymonik, and process-based models).
2. Service-specific management challenges and process mapping.
3. Efficiency assessment metrics and the theoretical frameworks supporting the authors’ original model.

Key Takeaways
The final section of the book is designed to bridge the gap between abstract management theory and practical application. By including an Annex of empirical evidence and a detailed Index, the authors ensure that researchers and practitioners can audit the logic behind the proposed quality cost matrix. The goal of this material is to provide the "credence" mentioned in the introduction, allowing readers to replicate or adapt the study’s methodology within their own service organizations.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Why have service enterprises historically struggled to adopt common quality cost accounting models?
options: Existing models were designed primarily for manufacturing processes.
Service enterprises lack quantifiable quality metrics.
Regulatory bodies prohibit quality cost accounting in service sectors.
Service companies prioritize customer satisfaction over financial efficiency.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that quality cost accounting has historically been used mainly in production enterprises due to the lack of models appropriate for service enterprises.

questions.question: What is the primary management goal for implementing the quality cost calculation model proposed by the authors?
options: To comply with international tax law requirements.
To replace traditional managerial accounting systems.
To assess the efficiency of an enterprise's management systems.
To eliminate all costs associated with service failures.
questions.explanation: The authors define the book's objective as offering a concept of using quality cost calculation specifically as a tool for assessing the efficiency of management systems.

questions.question: How does this study approach the challenges of quality management in service industries?
options: By applying manufacturing models directly to service operations.
By creating a model that accounts for the specific process-oriented nature of services.
By focusing strictly on philosophical definitions of quality.
By rejecting the use of budgets in quality control.
questions.explanation: The authors address the specificity of service enterprises—such as distinct service processes—and develop a dedicated model including process matrices and budgets tailored to those activities.

questions.question: According to the text, what determines whether a product or service possesses 'good' quality?
options: Strict adherence to ISO 9000 certification regardless of user feedback.
The degree to which inherent characteristics meet defined requirements.
The amount of financial investment allocated to market research.
The lowest possible production cost achievable.
questions.explanation: The text references the ISO 9000:2015 standard, which defines quality as the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object meets requirements.

questions.question: What is a 'process matrix of quality costs' in the context of the authors' model?
options: A marketing report used to attract new service clients.
A legal document outlining penalties for service failures.
A tool specifically developed for the authors' quality cost accounting model.
A software application required by external auditors.
questions.explanation: The text states that the process matrix of quality costs and the process budget of quality costs are tools specifically created to support the authors' model.

cards.front: What is the primary purpose of using 'quality cost calculation' in service enterprises?
cards.back: To assess the efficiency of their management systems and maintain competitiveness.

cards.front: Why has quality cost accounting historically been applied more in production enterprises than service sector companies?
cards.back: Because of a lack of models for quality cost accounting that are specifically appropriate for the operational nature of service enterprises.

cards.front: What is the primary objective of the book 'Quality Management and Accounting in Service Industries' by Sadkowski and Jedynak?
cards.back: To present an original concept of using quality cost accounting as a tool for assessing the efficiency of management systems in service enterprises.

cards.front: How does the PN-EN ISO 9000:2015 standard define 'quality'?
cards.back: The degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object meets requirements.

cards.front: What does 'the iceberg of quality costs' (Figure 1.2) suggest about measuring quality costs?
cards.back: It implies that many hidden or indirect quality-related costs exist below the surface that are not captured by standard accounting, similar to the submerged part of an iceberg.

cards.front: What are the two main parts that constitute the structure of the authors' book?
cards.back: The theoretical part (theoretical issues, models, and service specificity) and the empirical part (original model, methodology, and verification).

cards.front: Why is it important to identify, measure, and control quality-related costs?
cards.back: Because the ability to provide products or services that meet customer requirements competitively (in terms of quality, price, lead time) depends on them.

cards.front: What does the authors' 'process matrix of quality costs' provide?
cards.back: A tool for the model that helps map costs to specific phases of the process, such as planning, procurement, delivery, and sales.

cards.front: According to Deming, Juran, and Crosby, what does 'product quality' represent?
cards.back: The degree of freedom from defects and errors, conformity to requirements, or suitability for use or application.

cards.front: What does the 'modified Bernatene-Grün diagram' illustrate in this book?
cards.back: The procedure for conducting quality cost accounting in service enterprises.

slides.title: Quality Management and Accounting in Service Industries
bullets: Global competition requires improved management system efficiency.
Quality cost calculation helps assess and maintain competitiveness.
The book addresses the research gap for service companies.
Presents theoretical and empirical quality cost models (p. 2).
slides.notes: This lecture explores how quality cost accounting informs service management. It balances theoretical frameworks with practical, tested models.

slides.title: Introduction to Quality Cost Accounting
bullets: Quality accounting optimizes costs and ensures competitiveness.
Historically used in manufacturing; now vital for service sectors.
Service roles dominate modern economies and employment (p. 15).
Objective: A practical model for assessing management efficiency.
slides.notes: We introduce the shift from manufacturing focus to service applications. Quality accounting is presented as a strategic tool for organizational success.

slides.title: Defining Quality: From Philosophy to Practice
bullets: Historical roots span ancient philosophy to modern standards.
Quality Gurus defined quality as freedom from defects (p. 17-18).
ISO 9000:2015 defines it via inherent object characteristics.
Interdisciplinary nature makes a single definition difficult (p. 18).
slides.notes: Understanding quality starts with its philosophical evolution. We emphasize that quality is effectively measured by customer satisfaction and expectations.

slides.title: A Review of Existing Quality Cost Models
bullets: Literature review of existing quality cost accounting models.
Analysis of structural and activity-based cost models.
Essential for understanding current academic and industry perspectives.
Builds foundation for the proposed service-oriented model (p. 15-16).
slides.notes: Chapter 2 provides a critical review of existing literature. This sets the stage for developing a unique model specifically for services.

slides.title: Specificity of Service Enterprises
bullets: Examines the essence of services and service processes.
Summarizes service quality management and measurement methods.
Identifies specific determinants of quality costs in services.
Key component for tailoring management tools (p. 16).
slides.notes: Service-based businesses operate differently than manufacturing. We identify the unique drivers of quality costs in this specific economic sector.

slides.title: The Proposed Service Quality Model
bullets: Presents the authors' original quality cost accounting model.
Includes process matrices and budgets for service quality.
Uses a modified Bernatene-Grün diagram for accounting procedures.
Designed specifically for service-based enterprise structure (p. 16).
slides.notes: This chapter introduces the core conceptual framework. It offers practical tools, specifically the process matrix, for implementation in service sectors.

slides.title: Empirical Methodology and Verification
bullets: Conducts research across selected service enterprise case studies.
verifies model efficiency in real-world management systems.
Analyzes possibilities and limitations of the model.
Provides empirical evidence for economic practice (p. 2; p. 16).
slides.notes: We conclude by testing the model in real service environments. The results confirm the model's practical utility for improving management system efficiency.
